content:
Ryan, that’s a fair and pastoral question.

A couple of anchors first:
- God is indeed big enough to be honored by people who are honestly trying to read his Word and obey it, even when they land in different places on secondary matters.
- At the same time, we don’t edit Scripture to win respectability. The call to follow Christ includes social cost (1 Cor 1:18; Matt 5:11–12). But we also don’t add needless offense by insisting on what Scripture doesn’t actually require (Rom 14; Titus 3:9).

On “literal”: in the Christian tradition the “literal sense” means the author’s intended sense, taking genre and language conventions seriously—not a wooden, modern-scientific reading. Scripture often speaks phenomenologically: “sunrise,” “ends of the earth,” etc. That’s not error; it’s normal human speech accommodated to us. The Chicago Statement on Biblical Inerrancy explicitly affirms this.

About the firmament (raqia):
- The word can mean “expanse.” In Genesis 1 God calls the raqia “heavens,” and birds fly across it (Gen 1:20), which naturally reads as the sky. The “waters above” can be understood as the waters we see above us (clouds/precipitation) or as part of a grand, temple-like cosmic picture. Poetry can picture the sky as a hard surface (Job 37:18), but poetry trades in vivid images.
- Faithful, inerrantist readers have held different views: young-earth, old-earth, framework/analogical days, cosmic-temple readings. None of these require you to deny that Scripture is true. They differ on what, exactly, Genesis is intending to teach at the level of cosmology.

So yes: God can be honored either way—by the believer who says, “I think Genesis is using ancient-yet-true, phenomenological language to exalt God as Creator,” and by the believer who says, “I think Genesis describes a structure I can’t fully square with modern models, and I’m willing to bear that.” What matters is humble submission to what God actually meant to say, not to what will cost us least socially.

Two guardrails help keep us out of the ditch:
- Don’t make a secondary question a boundary of the faith. The gospel is of first importance (1 Cor 15:1–4). Unity in essentials, liberty in non-essentials, charity in all things.
- Don’t surrender convictions just to avoid embarrassment. If the text really compels you, bear the cost with a gentle, non-combative spirit (1 Pet 3:15; 2 Tim 2:24–25).

If you’re worried about “making Christianity unnecessarily hard,” remember: the cross is already a stumbling block. We should be careful not to add extra stumbling blocks of our own making, but we must not remove the ones God put there. The wise path is to major on what Scripture majors on and hold the rest with open-handed humility.

Practically, here are two faithful ways to speak about it:
- If you lean non-concordist: “I take Genesis as true in what it intends—God made and ordered everything. I don’t think the chapter is trying to give a modern scientific model, so I’m comfortable reading raqia as the sky/expanse in phenomenological terms.”
- If you lean strict literalist: “I trust Scripture’s description even when it puts me out of step. I don’t make this a test of fellowship, but I’m not embarrassed to stand with God’s Word.”

Either way, model careful reading, charity toward other Christians, and courage before a watching world. Ask the Lord for wisdom (James 1:5), stay tethered to the church’s core confession, and let the necessary offense be the beauty and lordship of Jesus—not our pet interpretations.
